item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This article explicitly mentions a GoI format article by name, as such, a crosslink to that article could easily be inserted.

These frequencies are produced by the human voice during the vocalization of songs by the band Constellation Starfish (a group affiliated with the Fifth Church) including singing, humming, or simply speaking the lyrics; a single word from the song "Old Chilly the Space Trucker", intoned as conceived by the composing artist, was found to produce enough energy to power SCP-2425 for approximately five hours.

To

These frequencies are produced by the human voice during the vocalization of songs by the band Constellation Starfish (a group affiliated with the Fifth Church) including singing, humming, or simply speaking the lyrics; a single word from the song "Old Chilly the Space Trucker", intoned as conceived by the composing artist, was found to produce enough energy to power SCP-2425 for approximately five hours.

If anyone has any other idea, feel free to suggest them below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of goofy, but works regardless. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, I think it fits well with pre-existing Fifithsm materials. But I am confused: why does it need to be routinely de-energized? If the object is an android that is powered by (and therefore recharging its battery through) songs of the Constellation Starfish, it should be permanently de-powered once the charge is used up, as long as no songs of the Constellation Starfish are played in its presence.

So why not lock it up in a sound-proof room with no recording equipments, and wait till its power runs out? It will be safe then.

Only explanation I can think of for this issue is that it recharges whenever the Constellation Starfish plays or whenever one of their songs are played anywhere, regardless of geological location of these performances. The problem is then, if the android is recharged every time a song from the Constellation Starfish is played, why does other musical performance of religious intends discharge it only when performed in its presence?

This really bothers me, if that's your intend I believe you should add some information that indicates this is the case; if not, I'd like to know the explanation. Neutral for now.

+Scorpion451:
Oops, my mistake, sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this article, I think it fits well with pre-existing Fifithsm materials. But I am confused: why does it need to be routinely de-energized? If the object is an android that is powered by (and therefore recharging its battery through) songs of the Constellation Starfish, it should be permanently de-powered once the charge is used up, as long as no songs of the Constellation Starfish are played in its presence.

So why not lock it up in a sound-proof room with no recording equipments, and wait till its power runs out? It will be safe then.

Only explanation I can think of for this issue is that it recharges whenever the Constellation Starfish plays or whenever one of their songs are played anywhere, regardless of geological location of these performances. The problem is then, if the android is recharged every time a song from the Constellation Starfish is played, why does other musical performance of religious intends discharge it only when performed in its presence?

This really bothers me, if that's your intend I believe you should add some information that indicates this is the case; if not, I'd like to know the explanation. Neutral for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really like the occasional tale or Scip where the Foundation has a win once in a while.

They so often get fisted to death and then pissed down the river, it gets to the point where it's difficult to believe the fuckers are still at it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I missed something, but why does it have to be powered off more than once? If its power source is music from a very particular band, wouldn't powering it off once and locking it in a soundproof room be enough to keep it from doing anything? Or does it gradually repower itself and have to be shut down again every so often? Could it power itself by singing to itself, and would that allow a feedback loop?

Upbeat skips have to be done very carefully, and this is close. I feel like its threat level needs to be a bit higher. Also, for containment procedures where something as easy as burning a replica of a book now and then suffices, the added singing seems a bit too optional to not be listed as that: it might make more sense to list the procedures as the book burning, with an added note that staff who enjoy singing things from musicals that meet the criteria may/should schedule in advance so that the session can be recorded in case of significant events or whatever.

I like this, but I feel like the positivity gets in the way of it fitting with the Foundation standard just a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having a footnote describing the nature of the site seems weird to me. In-universe, anyone at the site knows what sort of site it is, and any other Foundation personnel could easily look up the site in whatever official documentation the Foundation has. The footnote seems to be for the out-of-universe real-life reader, and I don't think that sort of thing should go directly into the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh heck, I was right! What did I win?

No, for real, it's how I saw the idea, and honestly made me laugh a bit in a good way, kind of a 'good for you, guys!' at the group tricking the Foundation into giving them something to relax and passing it as a super-important containment procedure!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This guy. This guy actually gets it. Bravo on figuring it out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, seeing all the comments, I seem to have understood this wrong.
I thought that the ONLY actually needed thing was burning the book.

The songs help, yes, but the book burning does it easier and quicker… so the only real reason to keep the singing around is FOR THE PEOPLE.

And they sent the request for containment inclding them 'cause they wanted a way to relax from their work and still feel they were doing something useful.

That was how I understood it, but I guess I'm wrong.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
borderline malicious in its intent

Ihpkmn, please let staff know if you feel that you're being treated unfairly, and refrain from calling people out in threads yourself.

I personally feel Kalinin was a bit harsh here, but this isn't the place to discuss it. For the record, Kalinin is a member of the Forum Criticism team, which I'm currently captain of. I'll speak with him if need be.

Please keep thread discussion from here on to the topic of the draft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And now you are just being purposefully pedantic. I acknowledged your criticism, I said that parts of it were valid, and you could have just moved on from that. But you just had to go and poke holes in my writing and deconstruct my statements. It's borderline malicious in its intent, outright annoying for anyone to do1, and unbecoming of your position on wiki staff.

I was wrong, and I acknowledged I was wrong. Now let's just move on.

Footnotes
1. Yes, I am counting myself in 'anyone'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The following Sunday, a sermon is to be given by a Foundation-appointed religious authority regarding either Exodus 20:3-6, the Book of Leviticus, a passage from the Book of Mormon, a reading from the Torah, a reading from the Qu'ran, or the Horizon Initiative's Universal Text, if available"

Exodus and leviticus are the second and third books of the torah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the main tenants of being a writer is "Write What You Know", and you seem to be getting offended because I'm doing just that.

I apologize if I've caused offense

Where did you get the idea that my criticism was because I was "offended"? I explained why I had issues with the exclusion of non-Abrahamic religions from the article with, you know, reasons.

You may not like that criticism and you may disagree with those reasons, which is fine. But don't ignore what I actually write and instead ascribe my motivations to stuff that you make up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved it! Very good job ihp! I'm a huge musical theater buff with, unfortunately, a very small wallet, so I've not seen any of these shows live. Now I really want to. Thank you for using musical theater in a scip and doing it (IMO) well.

Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, this is so cheesy but it's so fun and I enjoyed it. Fifthist stuff has always been my favorite and I always wanted to incorporate stuff like musicals into the Foundation but never been able to find a good angle.

[muffled Jacob Jacob Jacob Jacob and sooooooons chant in the distance]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The way it works is kind of like this:

(1)
\begin{equation} d= p-(n*t)/2 \end{equation}
  • d is the amount of power drained from SCP-2425.
  • p is the amount of total power before the drain.
  • n is the number of researchers singing the song.
  • t is the length of the song in minutes.

"Go Go Go Joseph" in all its parts lasts around 6 minutes, and requires about 15 researchers to perform: one for the narrator, one for Joseph, one for the Butler, one for the Baker, and the remainder for the chorus. It lasts about six minutes. Let's say that p=700 (units don't matter for the purpose of this equation).

So, it would be:

(2)
\begin{equation} d= 700-(6*15)/2 \end{equation}
(3)
\begin{equation} d= 700-(90)/2 \end{equation}
(4)
\begin{equation} d= 700-45 \end{equation}
(5)
\begin{equation} d= 655 \end{equation}

Yes, I seriously thought this out that much.

To answer your un-serious question:

  • Been a while since I've seen El Dorado, but it has to apply to a specific religion. El Dorado worked in broad strokes when it came to Maya/Inca/Aztec mythology, as opposed to specifics, at least from what I recall.
  • Life of Brian: Never seen it (and I've never seen Holy Grail either), so I dunno.
  • Hercules: maybe, if it was one of the ones talking about the Gods or Hercules themselves. "I Won't Say I'm In Love" probably wouldn't work.

E: realized I put "percent" in there by mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would any song from "The Life of Brian", "The Road to El Dorado" or Disney's "Hercules" work here, or is it only musical theater that counts?

All set aside, I'm starting to get inklings of a CotBG themed musical in my head now… Damnit, Ihp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This piece also falls into the mindset that a lot of religion-oriented pieces do around here that religion = Abrahamic religion.

You personally know of a lot fewer Buddhist pieces of musical theater, it's true. Does that mean that Foundation researchers who specialize in religious phenomena don't?

I searched for literally five hours to find a single reference to any non-Abrahamic piece of musical theater because I knew that this would eventually be a criticism that would come up eventually. The fact is: I'm not omniscient. If I don't know about it, I can't write about it, and therefore my characters don't know about it.

I could have made one up, but making up musicals based on Buddhist traditions- which I know very little about, apart from statistics on the religion in Japan and some of the history it is based on- would be more disrespectful to an entire culture than omitting them entirely. And for the record, guess how many pieces of Buddhist/Hindu musical theater I found?

Four Buddhist musicals, none of which I could mention in the article because the best information about them was in a scholarly article behind a paywall. Those four, for the record, are entitled:

  • Jewel of Tibet the Musical
  • Above the Full Moon
  • Prince Siddhartha the Musical
  • Magic Mirror the Musical

As for Hindu musicals? I could not find any. The closest I could find was a theatrical production based on the book Life of Pi by Yann Martel, which has themes from Hinduism, but also from Christianity and Islam (Just for the record, I could not find any Islamic musicals either.)

There is one religion that you overlooked that, looking back, I could have included: Shinto. They have a specific style of theater called Kagura which often involves musical performances, specifically dancing. I might have been able to mention that in an experiment log, but here's the thing: it wouldn't have worked anyway. Kagura dances are sacred to Shinto religion, often depicting scenes from their mythology, such as Amaterasu emerging from the cave or Susano-O slaying Orochi. And because they're sacred, like, say a hymn, as opposed to simply being spiritual and/or profane, they would have no effect on SCP-2425.

The fact is, I used musicals with a basis in Abrahamic faith because they are the ones I know. I've seen Dreamcoat at least twice in my life, both times with my now-passed away grandmother. The Prince of Egypt was literally one of the first animated films I ever rented1. I've memorized the lyrics to "If I Were a Rich Man" from Fiddler, and I own the film version of it with Chaim Topol playing Tevye. I'm planning on seeing The Book of Mormon when it comes to Toledo this December.

One of the main tenants of being a writer is "Write What You Know", and you seem to be getting offended because I'm doing just that. I love musical theater, and I wanted to convey a love for some of my favorite musicals in SCP form. I apologize if I've caused offense, but I feel the only thing I've done is sin of omission due to ignorance.

Footnotes
1. and I absolutely loved "Playing With The Big Boys", which is a musical number from that which has no mention of an Abrahamic God, only the Egyptian pantheon, and yes, it works on SCP-2425.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The average theologian or religious studies researcher may be more likely than the average person off the street to be familiar with Buddhist musicals, but in this part of the world, I'd say that's still somewhat esoteric knowledge. Besides, I'm not even sure if this SCP would have much in the way of religious phenomena researchers associated with it. The purpose is religious, yes, but the methods and function are not. As you note, the Fifthist connection is pretty tangential to this thing's core working.

Still, I'm not sure why this matters. Why bother searching out less culturally resonant Buddhist musicals when there's a perfectly fine selection of ones that would likely hold more appeal to the (presumably) Western researchers? Given that they only need do a performance once a month, and there's no indication that a musical loses its potency from overexposure, I don't see why the selection in the containment procedures isn't perfectly sufficient. There may well have been a test with non-Abrahamic musicals, but is that even worth bringing up?

I generally agree with your critique of this, and I even agree that the trend in religious SCPs to ignore non-Abrahamic religions is irritating. But for this SCP, I don't see any reason why the musicals in the containment procedures shouldn't just be Dr. Eldridge's five favorite religiously-themed musicals. And if those happen to be Abrahamic? That's just a reflection of the culture he grew up in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The other thing I'm upvoting for is that the researchers getting all happy and wanting to do these musicals wasn't an anomalous effect, just human nature + a horribly depressing job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also upvoting mainly for the Book of Mormon reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would the Foundation use the Fifthist Church format to write up a skip being contained by the Foundation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In fairness, I know of a lot fewer Buddhist pieces of musical theater.

You personally know of a lot fewer Buddhist pieces of musical theater, it's true. Does that mean that Foundation researchers who specialize in religious phenomena don't? Even allowing for the supposition that this version of the Foundation draws from a largely Western talent pool, don't you think there might be a few people on hand who might themselves have that kind of knowledge?

And if the Abrahamic ones are working perfectly well, why bother searching out more?

If this is an outfit that has a breadth of religious experience (which is implied in the doctor's commentary), one would think that maybe some other influences might have showed up when they were drafting the procedures. Unless this thing is indeed only affected by Abrahamic religious references, which if true seems like it would merit a mention somewhere in the document.

item.title: Re: Hello, My name is Elder Ihp, and I would like to share with you this most amazing commentary.
item.content:encoded: Yep, with them bulleted and indented like this, it looks like a researcher working within Foundation parameters and simply highlighting that within special containment procedures two procedures are necessary. I think it's an elegant solution, and it doesn't bother me (someone who tends to think working within the simple format limitation is important) at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In fairness, I know of a lot fewer Buddhist pieces of musical theater. And if the Abrahamic ones are working perfectly well, why bother searching out more?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is weighed down by a number of gimmicks that, rather than reinforcing or adding to the main narrative of the article, come across as unsuccessfully attempting to obfuscate the fact that the core body-snatcher subject of the piece is a bit thin.

The Fifthist angle is GoI shoehorning writ large. There's nothing particularly evocative of that organization in what's being written about here, except perhaps the use of a celebrity in the entertainment industry; it's an automaton thing that compels people to convert to a religion. It really could have been any old weird cult or whatever that assembled this (if we're GoI shopping, this might even be more of a CoTBG-type thing), and the Fifthist flavor is provided entirely by the use of crosslinks. I'm not against the notion of crosslinking in general, but there should be some sort of proper context for the pieces being brought into the narrative, otherwise it tends to end up as coattail riding (especially in the case of Star Signals, here).

This piece also falls into the mindset that a lot of religion-oriented pieces do around here that religion = Abrahamic religion. The doctor in the commentary talks all this business about how all gods are real, but the containment uses Abrahamic religions exclusively. I understand that 99% of us write from a Western perspective, but I would imagine that a unit dedicated entirely to religious phenomena might at least acknowledge the existence and possible usefulness of stuff like Hinduism or Buddhism, what with the billions of people on Earth who are not Christians, Muslims, or Jews.

Several commenters have cited the wacky commentary, and that angle didn't work for me. I don't care for silliness in what is supposed to be an official, clinical document, and it clashes greatly with the whole "rare success in a field of disasters" feel that you're trying to give this. I'm not particularly charmed by the "lol we have to do songs from a wacky and topical musical for containment" bit, but even for those who like whimsy and such in SCP articles, I can't imagine juxtaposing that with references to researchers killing themselves is doing the piece any favors.

This just has too many fundamentally flawed precepts in the original concept to work for me, and those flaws are ultimately borne out in the execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good and interesting, to the point that I don't even mind the small format screw.

Good job +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think djkaktus hit the nail on the head about this getting a free ticket on the absurdity by being exactly the sort of flaw the Fifthists would overlook:
Acolyte: Um, sir, what happens if the foundation surrounds our memetically powered robot with a choir singing religious-themed showtunes?
Head of Simulacrum Project: stares at acolyte in silence
Acolyte: Hey, it could…er…shutting up now.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You used book of Mormon in an SCP before I could and I hate you for it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor problem:

If individuals that have not undergone Level-2 Inoculation against Memetic Vector 2425-01, they will

If those individuals do what?

item.title: Re: Hello, My name is Elder Ihp, and I would like to share with you this most amazing commentary.
item.content:encoded:
First and foremost: before people say anything about the Special Containment Procedures being blank: they aren't. I just split them into the Object and Informational Containment Procedures subheadings. … If there's enough outcry about it, I'll take it down and standardize it.

What if you used bullet points to indent the sub-containment procedures? Visually, it would make it clearer that the other procedures are extensions of the parent. Like so:

Special Containment Procedures:

  • Object Containment Procedures:

SCP-2425 is to be kept in a 10.5m x 8.5m x 5m containment cell in Reliquary Site-251. The cell is to be outfitted with one stage me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like two separate SCP concepts smushed together: the robot actor that subliminally recruits people for an anomalous religion and a religious entity that loses power when other religions are celebrated in a joyous manner. They fit together, but only barely.

I think this would much stronger as two fully developed skips instead of a single disjoined one, but there was still enough good stuff here for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, right. Whoops. I've seen it, and not that long ago. Not sure how I got that confused…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Angels in America, while very good, does not count as a musical. At least the original play isn't; there was an opera made out of it a while later, which I found out by Googling it just now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why not include Angels in America? It's a musical with religious themes. Weird enough ones it might be Fifthist itself, but in a good way!

EDIT: Never mind. Ihp is right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had a feeling that this day would come; just didn't think that it would be so soon. Just because the GOI's (for the most part) have their own formats now doesn't mean we can't use the groups in skips.

That said, I didn't particularly like this article. The tone and sloppy formatting of the changes to the article format clinched the downvote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm ok with this. It's still an item documented by the Foundation. That being said, I might see if I can't do a Fifth version of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Esko feels one way about this, and I feel another.

Someone finally made LOLFoundation acceptable. Sort of.

I'm only disappointed that you failed to utilize the brand new Fifthist Church template. Why else did anyone go through the effort of creating them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked. Nuff said in chat already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminding researchers to read from a holy text instead of a musical book?

I honestly thought I had eliminated that in a previous draft after someone said it was too lulzy, and thought they were right; nobody else pointed it out until just now. Fixing it now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Esko is right about the lolfoundation business, and I can agree with that. I'm having a hard time not feeling biased, but I think I do legitimately like it. The it makes sense that the containment procedures are zany because that's the exact kind of thing that Fifthists might overlook.

My only serious point of issue is the usage of "android". I feel like that isn't something the Foundation would use, but it isn't enough of a sticking point to sway my opinion. +1

Edit: Who am I kidding. This is the first time something I wrote has been referenced. I'm absolutely beaming over here.

item.title: Hello, My name is Elder Ihp, and I would like to share with you this most amazing commentary.
item.content:encoded:
First and foremost: before people say anything about the Special Containment Procedures being blank: they aren't. I just split them into the Object and Informational Containment Procedures subheadings. The reason I did this is simple: the OCP is meant to say how you keep an object in containment. The ICP1 is what you say in the event the object somehow leaks from containment. This is especially useful in, say, memetic SCPs. If there's enough outcry about it, I'll take it down and standardize it.2

The original idea I had was based off of a certain actor that's been in movies with names similar to Operation: Implausible, Clash of the Planets and Precipice of the Future. My mother had always said his acting was robotic and stiff, so I decided to make an expy of him that was an actual robot. Then, I got talking to Roget about this idea, and saw that Constellation Starfish for the contest was all about music. So, I got to thinking: what if the robot was powered by music, and it spiraled out from there. This was originally meant to be a character study in the actor himself, but, much like the star of Operation: Implausible, I felt that his personality would be too fake and robotic to work.

Thanks to everyone on chat who looked over this, special thanks to Roget for helping me get this off the ground. Enjoy your silly Fifthist skip.

Footnotes
1. Unfortunate acronym, I know.
2. No I won't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interested in the base idea here, but there's so much lolfoundation here that it's hard to breathe through it all. Reminding researchers to read from a holy text instead of a musical book? Singing and dancing to Andrew Lloyd Webber? More importantly, the need to make little asides and footnotes about "isn't this zaaaaaaaaany?" is what breaks suspension of disbelief for me. +1 for idea, -1 for execution, neutral vote overall.
